questions.question: What does @RestController add compared to @Controller?
choices: It combines @Controller with @ResponseBody so every return value is serialized to the response body (typically JSON), removing the need to wrap returns in views.
It opts into reactive request handling.
questions.explanation: @RestController = @Controller + @ResponseBody. Every method's return value bypasses view resolution and is serialized to the response body via the configured message converters (Jackson by default).

questions.question: Which Spring binding annotation matches ASP.NET Core's [FromBody]?
questions.explanation: @RequestBody binds the HTTP request body, typically deserialized from JSON via Jackson. @PathVariable matches [FromRoute], @RequestParam matches [FromQuery], and @RequestHeader matches [FromHeader].

questions.question: You want POST /api/orders to return 201 Created with a Location header pointing to the new resource. Which is the idiomatic Spring approach?
choices: Return the saved order directly; Spring auto-detects creation.
Return ResponseEntity.created(URI).body(saved), where URI is built via ServletUriComponentsBuilder or a hard-coded path.
Use @ResponseStatus(HttpStatus.CREATED) on the method; the Location header is added automatically.
questions.explanation: ResponseEntity.created(URI) sets 201 plus the Location header. @ResponseStatus alone sets the status code but does NOT add a Location header. ServletUriComponentsBuilder.fromCurrentRequest() builds the Location relative to the request, avoiding hard-coded base paths.
